stem: Write the quotient \( \dfrac{([[a]]) + ([[b]])i}{([[c]]) + ([[d]])i} \) in rectangular form \( x + yi \). What is the result?
solution: Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate \( ([[c]]) - ([[d]])i \): the denominator becomes \( ([[c]])^{2} + ([[d]])^{2} \), and after simplifying the quotient equals \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the numerator after multiplying by the conjugate but forgetting to divide by \( c^{2}+d^{2} \).
This negates the imaginary part, as if the conjugate applied to the answer instead of the denominator.
This swaps the real and imaginary parts of the correct quotient.
This flips the sign of the real part only.
This is off by one in the real part.
This is off by one in the imaginary part.

stem: Simplify \( \dfrac{([[a]]) + ([[b]])i}{([[c]]) + ([[d]])i} + \left(([[e]]) + ([[f]])i\right) \) and write the result in rectangular form \( x + yi \).
solution: First rationalize \( \dfrac{([[a]]) + ([[b]])i}{([[c]]) + ([[d]])i} \) by multiplying by the conjugate \( ([[c]]) - ([[d]])i \) over itself, then add \( ([[e]]) + ([[f]])i \); the sum simplifies to \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is only the quotient term, forgetting to add \( ([[e]])+([[f]])i \).
This is only the added number, forgetting to include the quotient.
This incorrectly adds the added number's parts into the numerator before dividing, instead of dividing first.
This negates the correct sum (a sign slip).
This is off by one in the real part.
This is off by one in the imaginary part.

stem: Simplify \( \dfrac{([[a]]) + ([[b]])i}{([[c]]) + ([[d]])i} - \dfrac{([[e]]) + ([[f]])i}{([[g]]) + ([[h]])i} \) and write the result in rectangular form \( x + yi \).
solution: Rationalize each quotient separately by multiplying by its own denominator's conjugate over itself, then subtract the two rectangular-form results; this simplifies to \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is only the first quotient, forgetting to subtract the second.
This is only the second quotient (with the wrong sign), forgetting the first.
This adds the two quotients instead of subtracting the second from the first.
This negates the correct difference (a sign slip).
This is off by one in the real part.
This is off by one in the imaginary part.

stem: For what real value of \( t \) is the quotient \( \dfrac{([[p]]) + ti}{([[c]]) + ([[d]])i} \) a real number (i.e. has imaginary part equal to \( 0 \))?
solution: Multiplying by the conjugate, the imaginary part of the quotient is proportional to \( t([[c]]) - ([[p]])([[d]]) \). Setting this to \( 0 \) gives \( t = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign on the correct expression.
This swaps the roles of \( c \) and \( d \) in the proportionality relation.
This drops the factor of \( p \) coming from the numerator's real part.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
